title: What is an Array? 📚
description: Store multiple items in consecutive memory.

slides.title: The List of Data 📋
slides.content:
Imagine you need to store the scores of 1,000 players. Creating variables like `score1`, `score2`, ..., `score1000` is impossible.

An **Array** allows you to store a massive list of data under a single variable name.

slides.title: Creating an Array
slides.content: To create an array in C, specify the type of data, the name, and the number of items it will hold inside square brackets `[]`.

quizData.question: What is wrong with this array declaration in C? `int numbers(10);`

options.text: Arrays must be declared using square brackets `[]`, not parentheses `()`.
You cannot store numbers in an array.
10 is too large for an array.

quizData.explanation: C strictly uses square brackets `[]` to declare and access arrays. Parentheses are for checking conditions and passing function parameters.

slides.title: Zero-Indexed 0️⃣
slides.content:
This is crucial: Computers start counting at ZERO, not 1!

If you have an array `ages = {25, 30, 18}`, `25` is at position **0**, `30` is at position **1**, and `18` is at position **2**.

These positions are called **Indices** (Index).

slides.title: Accessing Elements
slides.content: Use the square brackets `[]` with the index number to read or change a specific value inside the array.

quizData.question: If `int values[5];` represents an array with 5 slots, what is the index of the absolutely LAST element in the array?
quizData.explanation: Because we start counting at 0, the elements are at 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4. There is no index 5!
